concepts.lesson: Marginalisation refers to the process by which certain groups in society are pushed to the edges and denied equal access to resources, opportunities and power. These groups often face discrimination and social exclusion. Marginalised communities may struggle to access education, employment and social services.
concepts.real_example: Some tribal communities face marginalisation because they have limited access to education and jobs.
key_points: Certain groups are pushed to the margins of society

concepts.lesson: In India, some communities such as Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs), and other disadvantaged groups have historically faced discrimination and marginalisation. These communities often experience social, economic and educational disadvantages.
concepts.real_example: Scheduled Tribes living in remote areas may have fewer schools and hospitals.
key_points: SCs and STs are examples of marginalised groups
Often lack access to basic facilities

concepts.lesson: The Indian Constitution provides protection to marginalised communities through various laws and rights. It guarantees equality before the law and prohibits discrimination based on caste, religion, gender or place of birth. These provisions aim to create a fair and just society.
concepts.real_example: The Right to Equality ensures that all citizens are treated equally under the law.

concepts.lesson: Fundamental Rights in the Constitution help protect people from unfair treatment and discrimination. They ensure that every citizen has equal opportunities and freedom. These rights are especially important for protecting marginalised communities.
concepts.real_example: The Right to Equality prevents discrimination based on caste.

concepts.lesson: The government has introduced several laws to protect marginalised communities from discrimination and violence. These laws aim to ensure justice and equality by punishing those who violate the rights of disadvantaged groups.
concepts.real_example: The Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act protects SC and ST communities from discrimination and violence.

concepts.lesson: The government plays an important role in reducing marginalisation by introducing policies and programs that promote equality. These include educational support, job reservations and welfare schemes aimed at improving the lives of disadvantaged communities.
concepts.real_example: Reservation in education and government jobs helps marginalised communities get opportunities.

concepts.lesson: Social justice means creating a society where all individuals are treated fairly and have equal opportunities. It ensures that marginalised groups receive protection and support so that they can live with dignity and participate fully in society.
concepts.real_example: Providing scholarships to disadvantaged students promotes social justice.

concepts.lesson: Encouraging participation of marginalised communities in political and social life helps strengthen democracy. When these groups are involved in decision-making, their voices and concerns are represented in policies and governance.
concepts.real_example: Reserved seats in local government allow SC and ST representatives to participate in governance.
